title: Landscaping Services

narrative.executive_view: NAICS 561730 covers landscape care and maintenance, landscape installation, and businesses that combine those services with design or hardscape work; pure landscape architecture is outside the code [S1]. The attractive acquisition case is a route-dense, maintenance-heavy regional operator, not the whole code indiscriminately. AI can remove a meaningful share of estimating, scheduling, customer-service, sales-support, bookkeeping, and reporting work, but most wage dollars remain in outdoor physical roles. The result is a modest labor-productivity channel paired with a stronger consolidation channel, durable end demand, and real but execution-dependent multiple expansion.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: The dominant occupation is grounds maintenance, whose tasks include mowing, edging, pruning, planting, applying treatments, and operating outdoor tools [S2]. Those tasks are not treated as replaceable by software. The modeled AI surface is concentrated in clerical work, bookkeeping, administrative support, sales support, route and crew planning, proposal drafting, customer communications, and parts of management analysis. Some supervisory documentation and field routing can be automated, while in-person quality control, safety, travel, equipment handling, and customer escalation remain human. Robotic mowing or autonomous equipment would be a separate capital-and-field-automation thesis and is deliberately excluded from the AI labor fractions.
narrative.value_capture: BrightView reports recurring annual maintenance contracts that typically run one to three years but can generally be cancelled on 30 to 90 days' notice, alongside noncontract and project revenue [S4]. That structure delays immediate pass-through of back-office savings, but renewal competition, rebids, and low switching frictions limit permanent retention. Route density, local reputation, crew availability, contract quality, and account relationships create some moat; fragmentation and comparatively low entry barriers limit it. The distribution and moat inputs therefore assume partial, not complete, capture of AI-driven labor savings.
narrative.adoption_timing: Aspire's 2025 industry survey says 93% of respondents use some software while more than three quarters do not currently use AI [S3]. That supports a current effective-adoption estimate below one quarter, but the survey is vendor-sponsored, practice definitions are broad, and software usage is not the same as measurable labor substitution. Adoption should arrive through existing field-service, CRM, estimating, accounting, and communication platforms. A five-year base case to 50% effective adoption allows for fragmented buyers, workflow integration, field-data quality, training, and uneven owner sophistication.
narrative.consolidation_economics: The consolidation thesis is stronger than the direct AI thesis. LM150 regional rankings show a broad set of sizable landscape platforms [S6], and United Land Services reports 25 acquisitions since 2020 while growing from roughly $40 million to $285 million of revenue [S7]. A buyer can create value through route density, shared branches, procurement, fleet utilization, centralized selling and administration, and disciplined tuck-ins. The selected 5.0x entry and 8.0x exit are inside a 2026 secondary-market estimate of roughly 4-8x for private landscape services and 7-10x for PE-backed regional platforms [S5]. Those figures are indications rather than a disclosed transaction dataset, and the exit case requires genuine scale and professionalization.
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is DURABLE. Customers still require recurring vegetation control, property appearance, safety, irrigation, seasonal cleanup, and installation work, and BLS projects grounds-maintenance employment growth rather than disappearance [S2]. AI may make contractors more efficient but does not eliminate the physical service. Weather, drought, water restrictions, housing and commercial construction cycles, labor availability, and discretionary enhancement work affect mix and volatility; they do not imply that the underlying maintenance need melts away.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the time required for fragmented operators to turn AI features into effective labor substitution, the owner-age and succession profile, and whether a target can earn a platform exit multiple. The deterministic establishment-band count uses a broad margin proxy and excludes 20-99 employee firms, so it is a sizing screen rather than a census of $1-10 million EBITDA candidates. The occupation mix is available only at the five-digit 56173 level. The same route density and centralization mechanisms can improve both operations and exit quality; their use across the operating and consolidation narratives is disclosed to avoid treating one improvement as two independent facts.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition — 561730 Landscaping Services
sources.what_it_supports: Exact six-digit industry scope, included combinations of care, maintenance, installation, design, and hardscape work, and exclusions.

sources.title: Grounds Maintenance Workers
sources.what_it_supports: Physical task content of the dominant field occupation and projected continued employment demand.

sources.title: Defining Where Technology Meets Opportunity: Aspire's 2025 Landscape Industry Report
sources.what_it_supports: Industry-survey evidence that software use is broad but current AI use remains below one quarter of respondents.

sources.title: BrightView Holdings, Inc. 2025 Annual Report
sources.what_it_supports: Landscape-maintenance contract duration and cancellation terms, service mix, operating economics, and cost structure of a large scaled operator.

sources.title: Landscape Market Monitor — Public Landscape & Lawn Care Valuations
sources.what_it_supports: April 2026 secondary estimates for private landscape-services and PE-backed regional-platform valuation ranges.

sources.title: LM150: 2025 list by region
sources.what_it_supports: Evidence of numerous scaled regional landscape operators and market breadth by region.

sources.title: United Land Services builds a $285M powerhouse through 25 acquisitions
sources.what_it_supports: Named consolidator activity, acquisition count, growth, and density-led acquisition rationale.

sources.title: Most Small Business Owners Aren't Prepared for Succession
sources.what_it_supports: Broad U.S. small-business evidence on expected retirements and lack of succession preparation; used only as a low-quality proxy.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Fractions estimate task-level software substitution within each supplied occupation. Physical field execution, travel, safety, equipment use, and in-person supervision remain outside the estimate; administrative production, analysis, coordination, and communication receive higher fractions.
ai_replaceable_share.plausible_range: Approximately 8%-19% of total wage-weighted work after deterministic role weighting.
caveats: The source evidence describes tasks and adoption but does not provide measured occupation-level AI substitution rates.
The supplied OEWS role mix is at five-digit NAICS 56173, not exact six-digit 561730.
Robotic mowing, autonomous vehicles, and other physical-capital automation are excluded.

role_judgments.rationale: Landscaping and groundskeeping is overwhelmingly physical outdoor work; only small documentation and routing fragments are software-substitutable.
Crew scheduling, work-order review, reporting, and basic planning can be assisted, while field supervision, safety, inspection, and escalation remain human.
Tree trimming and pruning require site judgment, climbing or lift work, tool control, and safety execution; little direct task time is text-based.
General managers can automate reporting, forecasting, document drafting, and analytical preparation, but retain personnel, customer, capital-allocation, and accountability work.
Routine office records, forms, status updates, and data entry are highly exposed, with exception handling and controls remaining.
Lead research, proposal drafting, CRM updates, and follow-up can be automated, while relationship development, site walks, and negotiation remain human.
Treatment recommendations and records can be assisted, but chemical application, site diagnosis, compliance, and equipment handling remain physical and accountable.
Construction labor is manual, site-specific work; only a narrow set of instructions and documentation can be automated.
Scheduling, drafting, inbox handling, document preparation, and routine coordination are highly exposed, subject to human exception handling.
Transaction coding, reconciliation preparation, invoicing support, and routine bookkeeping are highly automatable, while review and controls remain.
The residual likely mixes additional field trades and service roles with smaller administrative functions, so it receives a conservative blended fraction.

pi_dist.rationale: Recurring contracts and project quotes permit a contractor to retain savings initially, but short cancellation windows, periodic rebids, and competition create medium-term pass-through.
caveats: BrightView is a large commercial operator and is not representative of every residential, installation, tree-care, or snow-services target.

pi_moat.rationale: Route density, branch presence, account relationships, crew availability, and service reliability offer some defense, but the market remains fragmented and contracts can be cancellable.
caveats: Moat varies sharply by route density, customer concentration, service mix, and local labor conditions.

t50_years.rationale: Existing software penetration provides a distribution channel, but present AI use remains below one quarter and effective workflow substitution requires integration, training, data quality, and owner trust.
caveats: The Aspire survey is vendor-sponsored and does not measure effective labor displacement or provide an exact NAICS 561730 sampling frame.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: More than three quarters of surveyed landscape businesses reported no current AI use, so 20% is a conservative practice-level estimate below the implied ceiling.
caveats: Any AI use is broader than effective adoption in the specific replaceable workflows modeled here.

historical_analogs.value: Back-office and field-service software reached broad use before AI: Aspire reports 93% using some software but fewer than one quarter using AI. The relevant analog is feature-by-feature diffusion through incumbent workflow platforms, with realized labor savings lagging feature availability.
historical_analogs.rationale: The contrast between broad software use and limited AI use supplies a directionally useful diffusion analog for a fragmented service industry.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Three to eight years for majority effective adoption, rather than immediate substitution.
caveats: This is a cross-sectional survey comparison, not a measured historical time series.

selection.rationale: A broad small-business survey finds 40% of owners expect to retire within ten years; 30% is used as a restrained proxy for current owners age 60 or older.
caveats: The source is not landscaping-specific and retirement intent does not map exactly to age 60 or older.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: The broad survey documents widespread absence of complete succession preparation among owners approaching retirement, used here only as a directional small-business succession signal.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: Documented broadly; exact NAICS applicability uncertain.
caveats: No exact NAICS 561730 survey of succession readiness was found, so this is an explicitly declared proxy.

active_consolidators.rationale: The LM150 identifies many scaled regional groups, and United Land Services alone reports 25 acquisitions; twelve is a conservative count of visibly scaled buyers or platforms plausibly active in tuck-ins.
caveats: The LM150 is a revenue ranking, not a live transaction database, and some listed groups may not be active acquirers in every period.

buy_mult.rationale: The base entry multiple sits within the cited 4-8x range for private landscape-services companies and reflects a maintenance-heavy regional add-on rather than a platform.
caveats: The source is a secondary advisory estimate, not a disclosed-deal comp set, and its company-size bands are not identical to the $1-10 million EBITDA screen.

exit_mult.rationale: Eight times EBITDA is near the lower-middle of the cited 7-10x PE-backed regional-platform range and assumes successful density, recurring maintenance mix, management depth, and reporting quality.
caveats: Exit multiple expansion is not automatic and could disappear if the asset remains owner-dependent, project-heavy, concentrated, or subscale.

pricing_structure.value: Maintenance is commonly sold through recurring annual service contracts, while enhancements and installations are job-priced; a large commercial operator reports typical one-to-three-year maintenance terms that are generally cancellable on 30 to 90 days' notice.
pricing_structure.rationale: The mix of recurring contracts and project quotes makes savings sticky inside a term but exposes them to rebids, renewal pressure, and customer switching.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: From short residential arrangements and cancellable commercial contracts to multi-year institutional agreements; project work remains separately quoted.
caveats: BrightView's commercial mix is broader and more scaled than a typical lower-middle-market target.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Yes. The direct AI opportunity is limited by the physical wage base, while adoption is gradual; the separate consolidation thesis rests on route density, shared overhead, and buyer-market evidence rather than inflating task replaceability.

terminal_value.justification: Recurring property upkeep and outdoor physical execution remain necessary, and the occupation evidence describes tasks software cannot perform directly [S2]. Cyclic installation and enhancement work do not overturn the durable maintenance base.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No undisclosed proxy is used. The five-digit occupation mix, broad service-margin band, broad owner-succession survey, vendor adoption survey, secondary valuation ranges, and revenue-ranking consolidator count are all explicitly identified.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Contract terms delay immediate price adjustment, but cancellation rights, renewals, rebids, and fragmented competition are expected to deflate retained savings over time; this is reflected in partial pi_dist and pi_moat values.

dataset_mismatch.description: The deterministic labor share and establishment data use different source vintages; the occupation mix is five-digit NAICS 56173 rather than exact 561730. The establishment screen uses a 15.65% broad business-and-consumer-services margin proxy and only the 100-499 employee band, so it is not a direct count of companies with $1-10 million EBITDA.
sensitivity: A lower true margin would reduce the number of establishments clearing the EBITDA floor.
Some 20-99 employee firms may clear $1 million EBITDA and are omitted, while some 100-499 employee establishments may fall below it.
A six-digit occupation mix could change the wage-weighted AI share if installation, maintenance, tree-care, and administrative mixes differ materially.

market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Regional maintenance-heavy contractors with recurring commercial, HOA, institutional, or residential accounts
market_boundary.selection_basis: The exact Census scope is broader than the preferred acquisition target [S1]. The dominant subsegment is selected because recurring maintenance and route density best support retention, integration, and platform scaling; multiples are not assumed to apply uniformly across project-heavy or specialty-only businesses.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A faster or slower path to majority effective adoption directly changes the adoption subscore and timing of labor benefits.
The broad proxy materially affects the succession component of buyability and should not be treated as exact industry demography.
Failure to achieve regional-platform quality would compress multiple arbitrage and materially reduce the consolidation score.

reviewer_note: Initial isolated v3.1.2 GOLDEN research packet. All score-driving URLs were reopened in the live-web session on 2026-07-21. Exact NAICS scope and both acquisition multiples are sourced; broad or secondary proxies are disclosed rather than presented as exact observations.
